coredata.dc:title: Cellulose nanocrystals: A versatile precursor for the preparation of different carbon structures and luminescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Souza, Débora Rosa da Silva
Mesquita, João Paulo de
Lago, Rochel Montero
Caminhas, Larissa Durães
Pereira, Fabiano Vargas
coredata.dc:description: The current study demonstrates that cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) that are assembled in different arrangements can be thermally decomposed under controlled conditions to produce different carbon nanostructures. The results from FTIR, Raman, TGA, SEM, TEM and potentiometric titration indicated that thermal decomposition in the 300–1000°C range produced carbons containing surface oxygen groups with different morphologies. The obtained results suggest that the obtained carbon structures are defined by the CNC precursor arrangement; i.e., CNCs organized as filaments produce carbon fibers, CNCs sheets result in carbon films and CNCs agglomerated as spheres will produce carbon spheres. Furthermore, the thermal decomposition of CNCs also produced water-soluble 4–8nm carbon dots (CDs), exhibiting photoluminescence emission as a function of the excitation energy that covered the blue-to-green visible light region.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose nanocrystals
Carbon nanostructures
Carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the eucalyptus CNC precursor used in this study.
FTIR spectra of CNCs, CNC300, CNC400, CNC500 (a) and CNC600, CNC800, CNC1000 (b).
Raman spectra of CNC300, CNC400, CNC500 and CNC600 (a) as well as CNC700, CNC800, CNC900 and CNC1000 (b).
Scanning electron microscopy images of the carbon nanostructures obtained at 300 (a, b, c), 600 (d, e, f) and 1000°C (g, h, i). (f) Shows a higher magnification image of (e).
TEM and HRTEM images of the carbon nanostructures obtained at 300 (a, b, c), 600 (d, e, f) and 1000°C (g, h, i).
Different CNC precursor structures and potential carbon-derived structures.
TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of CNC sample pyrolyzed at 600°C. The inset in (a) shows the particle size distribution.
UV absorption (a) and PL spectra at different wavelengths (b) for the CNC300 sample in water. The inset in (a) shows the CNC300 sample dispersed in ethanol and illuminated with a UV lamp (365nm).
Schematic representation of the proposed mechanism of CNC pyrolysis to form CDs.
Effect of the pyrolysis temperature on the total amount of acidic functional groups.

head.ce:title: Cellulose nanocrystals: A versatile precursor for the preparation of different carbon structures and luminescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Química—Universidade Federal de Minas Gerais, Av. Antônio Carlos 6627, Pampulha, Belo Horizonte, CEP 31270-901 MG, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Departamento de Química—Universidade Federal de Minas Gerais

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Química—Universidade Federal dos Vales Jequitinhonha e Mucuri, Rodovia MGT 367, Km 583, 5000 Alto da Jacuba Campus JK, Diamantina, CEP 39100-000 MG, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Departamento de Química—Universidade Federal dos Vales Jequitinhonha e Mucuri

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current study demonstrates that cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) that are assembled in different arrangements can be thermally decomposed under controlled conditions to produce different carbon nanostructures. The results from FTIR, Raman, TGA, SEM, TEM and potentiometric titration indicated that thermal decomposition in the 300–1000°C range produced carbons containing surface oxygen groups with different morphologies. The obtained results suggest that the obtained carbon structures are defined by the CNC precursor arrangement; i.e., CNCs organized as filaments produce carbon fibers, CNCs sheets result in carbon films and CNCs agglomerated as spheres will produce carbon spheres. Furthermore, the thermal decomposition of CNCs also produced water-soluble 4–8nm carbon dots (CDs), exhibiting photoluminescence emission as a function of the excitation energy that covered the blue-to-green visible light region.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of new substances, processes and materials from cellulose is currently of much interest both from an environmental perspective and from a socio-economic point of view due to its high availability and intrinsic renewable nature (Eyley and Thielemans, 2014; Habibi et al., 2010). In nature, cellulose chains are arranged into repeating crystalline structures to form microfibrils. These elementary microfibrils also aggregate to form larger fibers, which contain amorphous and crystalline regions (Klemm et al., 2011; Eichhorn et al., 2010; Zhang and Lynd, 2004).

Biomass, such as cellulose, which does not compete with the food supply, may be an excellent choice for the production of carbon materials. These carbon materials have wide potential application in different research and industrial areas due their unique properties, such as a high surface area, conductivity, porous structure and surface functional groups that allow various chemical modifications (Titirici et al., 2015; Baker and Baker, 2010).

Among these carbons materials, carbon dots (CDs) represent a new family of nanocarbons with a spherical shape and size less than 10nm (Baker and Baker, 2010; Sahu et al., 2012). These nanocarbons were accidentally discovered during the electrophoretic purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) when researchers looked beyond the SWCNTs and discovered a mixture of nanoparticles that exhibited photoluminescence behavior (Xu et al., 2004). Since their discovery, these very small particles have attracted much interest due to their high photoluminescence and good biocompatibility for biomedical applications compared to that of nanoparticles prepared with heavy metals (Puvvada et al., 2012; Du et al., 2014). Moreover, CDs are also considered emerging materials for energy-related and photocatalytic applications (Zhang et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2014) due to the possibility of tuning their emissions from the near-infrared region to the ultraviolet region.

Cellulose and lignocellulose-based biomass have been converted using pyrolysis and hydrothermal carbonization to produce carbon materials from renewable precursors with distinct properties (Wan et al., 2015; Guiotoku et al., 2009; Falco et al., 2011).

In general, the cellulose-derived carbon structures that have been described in the literature are composed of microspheres in the 2–5μm size range and have been obtained using different methods, such as chemical vapour deposition, pyrolysis and hydrothermal treatments (Zhao et al., 2013b; Sevilla and Fuertes, 2009). These cellulose-derived carbon spheres, which can be prepared in different sizes and with oxygenated functional groups on their surface, have attracted much attention due to their different applications, such as super capacitors, catalyst supports, and adsorbents (Titirici et al., 2015).

Cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs), which are also known as cellulose nanowhiskers (CNWs) or nanocellulose, consist of highly crystalline nanosize segments obtained from cellulose fibers when the amorphous regions are eliminated by acid hydrolysis (Eichhorn, 2011). These promising and advanced nanomaterials that are 100–250nm long and 4–10nm wide, have received considerable attention in recent years due to their remarkable features, such as excellent mechanical properties, low density and inherent renewable character, making them interesting candidates as reinforcing nanofillers for different polymers (Samir et al., 2005). Additionally, the CNCs can be easily prepared, present nanoscale sizes, differentiated morphology (high aspect ratio) and high surface areas.

In this study, CNCs have been investigated as precursors for the production of different carbon nanostructures using conventional pyrolysis. CNCs exhibit several advantages as precursors for the production of carbon structures including a relatively high fixed carbon yield and relatively low cost as well as the unique possibility for assembly into different morphologies (e.g., single nanoparticles, films, filaments or aggregates). Then, these different CNC assemblies can be thermally decomposed to produce specific carbon structures. Herein, for the first time, we report the use of CNCs as precursors for the production of very interesting nanostructured carbon dots (CDs).

Sulfuric acid hydrolysis of eucalyptus kraft wood pulp was performed according to a previously reported procedure with some modifications (de Rodriguez et al., 2006; Brito et al., 2012). First, 20g of bleached cellulose pulp was added to 160mL of 64wt% sulfuric acid under strong mechanical stirring at 55°C for approximately 30min. After hydrolysis, the dispersion was diluted in water and washed three times with deionized water using centrifugation. Dialysis against deionized water (until the dispersion reached a pH of ∼7) was used as the final purification step. The dispersions were ultrasonicated with an Ultrasonic Processor Cole Parmer CPX750 equipped with a microtip, and a stable suspension of CNCs was obtained after sonication for approximately 1min. The obtained aqueous suspension of CNCs (∼1%m/v) was freeze-dried to obtain a powder consisting of cellulose nanoparticles.

The pyrolysis of the freeze-dried CNCs was carried out in a tubular furnace under a nitrogen flow of 50mLmin−1 with a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1. A systematic study was performed at 300–1000°C, producing carbons materials (i.e., CNC300, CNC400, CNC500, CNC600, CNC700, CNC800, CNC900 and CNC1000, respectively). After pyrolysis, the obtained carbon materials were characterized as obtained or after dispersion (in different solvents) and centrifugation.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded using a FEI Tecnai G2-Spirit with an acceleration voltage of 120kV. The CNCs were deposited from an aqueous suspension onto a carbon-Formvar-coated copper TEM grid. The CNC samples were stained with a uranyl acetate solution to enhance the microscopy resolution. The carbon materials were deposited from aqueous dispersions on a continuous carbon film TEM grid.
Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was carried out using a Quanta 200 FEG-FEI microscope. The images were collected from the same samples that were deposited on a continuous carbon film TEM grid.

The Raman spectra were obtained using a Senterra system with a 633nm excitation wavelength. The measurements were made using a 20× objective lens with a laser power of 5mW, and an exposure times of 2–10s.

Potentiometric titration curves were determined at 25°C using a Metrohm 670 automatic titrator according to our previously described protocol (de Mesquita et al., 2010). In a typical procedure, 50mg of the sample was dispersed in an HCl solution in an electrochemical cell followed by titration with a NaOH solution (CO2 free). A nonlinear method was employed to fit the acid-base potentiometric titration data to determine the quantity of acid groups on the surface of the carbon.
Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed using a Perkin Elmer FTIR spectrometer (RX) in a frequency range of 4000–400cm−1. The samples were prepared using the KBr disk method.
The absorption spectra were obtained on a Varian Cary 100 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer.

The cellulose nanocrystals used in this study were rod-shaped nanoparticles that can be found isolated or as aggregates (Fig. 1 ). The average length and diameter of the CNCs, which were measured from several TEM images, were determined to be 140nm and 4–5nm, respectively. The TEM images also indicated the presence of CNCs bundles due to the high specific area as well as the strong hydrogen bonds between the cellulose nanoparticles.

The thermal behavior of the CNCs, which was investigated by TG analysis (see Supplementary material, Fig. S1), indicated an onset degradation temperature near 260°C and a residual fixed carbon of approximately 17wt%. However, regular cellulosic fibers begin to decompose at approximately 320°C but almost no fixed carbon was obtained (Supplementary material, Fig. S1).

The low thermal stability of the CNCs treated with sulfuric acid was systematically studied (Roman and Winter, 2004). According to these authors, the decrease in thermal stability of the CNCs is related to the presence of sulfate groups on the surface of the nanoparticles. These groups could facilitate dehydration reactions which would reduce the thermal stability of the CNCs. In addition, the relatively high fixed carbon yield found for CNCs (compared to cellulosic fibers) is most likely related to the presence of these sulfate groups on the CNC surface because these groups can act as flame retardants (Roman and Winter, 2004). This relatively high fixed carbon yield is an important characteristic for the production of carbon from CNCs. Moreover, the nanometer dimensions and possible CNCs assemblies can produce different carbon nanostructures. Based on these results, the thermal decomposition of CNCs was carried out at different temperatures ranging from 300 to 1000°C.
Fig. S2 shows photographs of the freeze-dried CNC precursor and the material obtained after pyrolysis of the nanocrystals at different temperatures.

Fig. 2 shows the FTIR spectra of the CNCs (precursor) and the carbon material obtained at different temperatures. The spectrum of the CNCs, which is provided for comparison to the spectra of the prepared carbon material, contained typical absorptions for cellulose I e.g., 3340cm−1 (OH stretching), 2900 and 1431cm−1 (CH stretching and −CH2 bending, respectively), 1050 and 1200cm−1 (saccharide structure) (Oh et al., 2005) and absorption at 1600cm−1 related to OH groups and water molecules adsorbed on the CNCs surface.

Upon thermal treatment at 300–500°C (CNC300, CNC400 and CNC500) the destruction of the saccharide structure was observed due to the appearance of new bands at 1600–1700cm−1 (CO) and 1157cm−1 (CO), which are most likely related to ethers, carboxylic acids and lactones. The broad band located at 3430cm−1 was due to −OH stretching, and the band at 1610cm−1 is characteristic of CC from aromatic structures. At temperatures higher than 600°C, most of the bands disappeared due to the thermal decomposition of oxygen functional groups (Haydar et al., 2000, Fig. 2b).

Table 1 shows the results obtained from the potentiometric titrations. For the carbon materials, the carboxylic functional groups have pKa<5, lactones have a pKa of 6–8, and phenolic groups have pKa>8 (Gorgulho et al., 2008; Kundu et al., 2008). Therefore, the material obtained at 300°C contained significant amounts of carboxyl functional groups compared to those of the samples treated at higher temperatures, which is related to the decomposition of carboxylic surface groups at temperatures higher than 300°C that resulted in the presence of only lactones and phenolic groups. A point of zero charge (PZC) of approximately 9–10 was obtained for samples at 600 and 900°C due to the decomposition of more acidic groups.

The Raman spectra of the carbons obtained at different temperatures (Fig. 3 ) contained intense absorptions at 1580cm−1 (G band) and 1340cm−1 (D band), indicating the presence of well-organized and more defective carbons, respectively. It is interesting to note that the relative intensity of the D and G bands (ID/IG) gradually increased from 0.61 (for CNC300) to 1.10 (for CNC1000). This result is most likely related to the decomposition of the cellulose, which was partially decomposed to less organized carbon that exhibited a pronounced D band.

The SEM images of the carbons obtained after treatment of the CNCs (Fig. 4 ) indicated the presence of different carbon structures, i.e. fibers (or rod-like carbons), sheets and isolated particles. Interestingly, the samples obtained at different temperatures exhibited similar morphologies. Careful observations of these SEM images suggested that the fiber-like structures are composed of welded fragments of the CNCs. These fragments primarily consisted of irregular rod-like particles that were 100–300nm in length. In addition, thin (few nm thick) large sheets (1–3μm) with fairly regular surfaces were observed. For example, the result in Fig. 4f, which shows a detailed image obtained from Fig. 4e, indicated that the sheets are formed by thin carbon layers. The SEM images of the carbons obtained at 1000°C indicated the presence of isolated particles with needle shapes (i.e., 100–200nm, Fig. 4g), sheets (Fig. 4h) and nanospherical aggregates (i.e., 30–50nm range, Fig. 4i).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (Fig. 5 ) provided more insight into these 50–150nm-long rod-like carbon nanostructures (Fig. 5a and b), and sheets (Fig. 5c). The rod-like particle images suggest the presence of fringes (Fig. 5b), indicating that these particles were formed by interior crystalline structures with a spacing of ∼0.33nm, which is typical of the 002 graphitic plan, and exterior amorphous carbon with a thickness of approximately 5nm (Fig. 5b). In addition, similar to that detected in the SEM images, a large quantity of grapheme-like sheets was observed (Fig. 5c and f).

For the samples obtained at 1000°C, rod-like structures were observed with highly ordered graphitic carbon (0.33nm fringes, Fig. 5g and HRTEM Fig. 5h) as well as 50–150nm spherical carbon nanostructures with an outer layer composed of amorphous carbon that eventually formed aggregates (Fig. 5i).

Although the mechanism for the formation of these carbon structures remains unclear, these CNCs may pack together via strong H-bonding interactions, and thermal decomposition may lead to welding of the carbon particles to form more complex structures, as shown in Fig. 6 .

The fibrous structures could be formed by the decomposition of filamentous aggregates of CNCs, and the film and spherically structured CNCs may result in the formation of carbon sheets and sphere-like agglomerated particles. This behavior is unique for CNCs because the pyrolysis of raw cellulose fibers (not from cellulose nanoparticles) primarily resulted in the production of the amorphous aggregates and in some cases spheroids in the 2–5μm size range (Falco et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2013b; Sevilla and Fuertes, 2009).

It is interesting to note that the obtained carbon particles can be dispersed in water due to the high concentration of oxygen surface groups. To isolate the small particles obtained from the pyrolyzed materials, the samples, which were dispersed in water or other organic solvents, were centrifuged at 10,000RPM for 20min, and a stable black suspension was obtained from the supernatant.

After centrifugation, TEM and HRTEM analyses indicated the presence of suspended 4–8nm spherically shape particles (Fig. 7 a). The size and distribution size of the carbon dots (CDs) obtained from the pyrolyzed samples at different temperatures were very similar.

The CDs exhibited interplanar distances of 0.32 and 0.25nm (Fig. 7b), which are similar to the lattice spacing of the 002 and 100 planes of graphite (Lu et al., 2009; Tian et al., 2009). This result indicates that the CDs exhibited a crystalline graphitic structure.

To investigate the optical properties of the as-prepared carbon dots and evaluate the effects of the pyrolysis temperature on these properties, UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) studies were also carried out. Fig. 8 shows typical UV–vis absorption (Fig. 8a) and PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 8b) for the CNC300 sample dispersed in water. The absorption spectrum in water contained an absorption band at 265nm, which was due to the aromatic π-π* transition of the conjugated CC band and the n–π* transition of the CO band (Zhao et al., 2013a). The inset in Fig. 8a shows a photograph of the CNC300 suspension in ethanol illuminated with a UV (365nm) source. The stable black suspension (under visible light) exhibited a clear blue luminescence under UV light. Under different wavelengths of excitation, the CDs exhibited a fluorescence emission that covered the blue-to-green visible light region due to their emissions being highly dependent on the excitation energy. The emission peak position shifted linearly as the excitation increased. Fig. 8b shows the PL spectra of CNC300 using light at different wavelengths for excitation nearly to 350nm. The CDs emit in the blue region when excited from 320 to 360nm because the photoluminescence varies from 415 to nearly 450nm.
The fluorescence quantum yields of the obtained CDs were calculated using the slope method (Sahu et al., 2012), with quinine sulfate (0.1mol/L in sulfuric acid as solvent) as the standard.

Four concentrations of each sample were prepared using water as the solvent (with an absorbance ranging from 0.01 to 0.1 at 340nm for all of the samples), and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at the same excitation wavelength (i.e., 340nm). The integrated fluorescence intensity as a function of absorbance for all of the samples was compared to the standard, and the following equation was used:
ϕ
C D
=
ϕ
S T
(
S
C D
S
S T
)
(
η
C D
2
η
S T
2
)
where
ϕ
C D
is the quantum yield of the carbon dots, S is the slope, n is the refractive index, ST is the standard and CD is the carbon dot sample.
The calculated quantum yield of the obtained CDs varied between 0.8 (for CNC300) and 1.64% (for CNC600).

Although the mechanism of CD formation remains unclear, their dimensions (i.e., 4–8nm) are much smaller than the CNC dimensions (i.e., 140nm in length and 5nm in diameter, Fig. 1). Therefore, these CDs were most likely formed during the thermal decomposition of the CNCs due to fragmentation of the cellulose structure into small pieces that carbonize to form the CDs. In addition, during thermal decomposition, the formation of a gas/liquid product may react/polymerize and carbonize to produce the CDs particles (Fig. 9 ).

The mechanism of cellulose depolymerization involves the formation of levoglucosan (LGA, Lin et al., 2009). LGA further undergoes dehydration to form different anhydrosugars. The anhydrosugars also react further to form several species/molecules, such as furfural-like compounds, ketones, acids and aldehydes.

The formation of carbon dots from banana juice, which contains carbohydrates, such as fructose and glucose, was described as a nuclear burst of aromatic aggregates after a critical concentration point for these aromatic moieties (De and Karak, 2013). These aromatic aggregates were formed after condensation or polymerization of the different molecules formed by the hydrothermal carbonization of the carbohydrates. In a similar fashion, different species may be formed after cellulose depolymerization and undergo condensation/polymerization to form carbon dots. More detailed studies are necessary to fully understand the CD formation mechanism from CNCs.

To the best of our knowledge, carbon dots were prepared from nanoparticles obtained from biomass, such as cellulose nanocrystals, for the first time. Moreover, the low quantum yield could be overcome using different strategies, such as the use of passivation or doping agents.
In this study, carbon nanostructures with different morphologies were obtained via pyrolysis of cellulose nanocrystals at different temperatures.

For the materials obtained at relatively low pyrolysis temperatures (300–500°C), the carbons contained a significant amount of oxygenated functional groups, and at high temperatures (600–1000°C), the amount of these oxygenated groups decreased.

The different carbons morphologies (e.g., fibers and sheets) were due to the strong H-bonding interactions between the CNCs, which can pack the nanoparticles together, and thermal decomposition, which can lead to welding of the carbon structures to form complexes structures. Based on the different morphologies and properties of the nanostructured carbons that were formed, these biomass-based carbon materials may find applications in different areas, such as in adsorption (e.g., gas separation and water purification), catalysis or energy storage.

Moreover, for the first time, we have prepared carbon dots (CDs) from cellulose nanocrystals using different pyrolysis temperatures. The as-prepared CDs consisted of graphitic and amorphous carbon with a size ranging from 4 to 8nm. The water-soluble and bio-based fluorescent carbon dots exhibited an absorption band at approximately 270nm and a photoluminescence emission covering the blue-to-green visible region, and their emissions are highly dependent on the excitation energy.

Given the results presented here, the preparation of CD using CNCs are under investigation in our research group using different strategies, such as hydrothermal carbonization and doping of the carbon dots, to enhance the quantum yield.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank Fapemig (CEX-APQ-00851-13 and EDT 02/2015 PPM IX) for financial support and Centro de Microscopia/UFMG for providing the images.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.indcrop.2016.04.073.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

A green and facile approach for the synthesis of water soluble fluorescent carbon dots from banana juice
Biobased nanocomposites from layer-by-layer assembly of cellulose nanowhiskers with chitosan
Sisal cellulose whiskers reinforced polyvinyl acetate nanocomposites

Microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization of lignocellulosic materials
Cellulose nanocrystals: chemistry, self-assembly, and applications
Regularities in the temperature-programmed desorption spectra of CO2 and CO from activated carbons
Nanocelluloses: a new family of nature-based materials
Thermal stability and reducibility of oxygen-containing functional groups on multiwalled carbon nanotube surfaces: a quantitative high-resolution XPS and TPD/TPR study
Kinetics and mechanism of cellulose pyrolysis
One-pot synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoribbons, nanoparticles, and graphene by the exfoliation of graphite in ionic liquids
Crystalline structure analysis of cellulose treated with sodium hydroxide and carbon dioxide by means of X-ray diffraction and FTIR spectroscopy
Synthesis of biocompatible multicolor luminescent carbon dots for bioimaging applications
Effect of sulfate groups from sulfuric acid hydrolysis on the thermal degradation behavior of bacterial cellulose
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Review of recent research into cellulosic whiskers, their properties and their application in nanocomposite field
The production of carbon materials by hydrothermal carbonization of cellulose
Nanosized carbon particles from natural gas soot
Fabrication of hydrophobic, electrically conductive and flame-resistant carbon aerogels by pyrolysis of regenerated cellulose aerogels
Vegetable-extracted carbon dots and their nanocomposites for enhanced photocatalytic H-2 production
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Toward an aggregated understanding of enzymatic hydrolysis of cellulose: noncomplexed cellulase systems
Graphene quantum dots: an emerging material for energy-related applications and beyond
Chemiluminescence of carbon dots under strong alkaline solutions: a novel insight into carbon dot optical properties
Carbon spheres obtained via citric acid catalysed hydrothermal carbonisation of cellulose
